user:
Patient: Lila Antoinette Beaufort
Gender: F
DOB: 2014-11-04
Location: New Orleans, LA 70112
Facility: Children's Hospital New Orleans (NPI: 1234567861)
Encounter: Admission, 2026-04-21 14:32

=== HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS ===
Lila is an 11-year-old previously healthy girl who returned 14 days ago from a 3-week family trip to Bucharest, Romania. Six days prior to admission she developed onset of high fever (Tmax 40.1C at home), followed by progressive worsening of a brassy cough, profuse coryza, and conjunctivitis with photophobia. Her mother brought her to the pediatrician on day 4 of illness when small white spots were noted inside her cheek opposite the lower molars. The pediatrician noted the lesions and referred them urgently to our facility. Over the past 24 hours, a cephalocaudal maculopapular rash has developed, beginning at the hairline and behind the ears, and now extending across the trunk and proximal extremities. Lila has had reduced oral intake and her urine output has decreased per her mother's report.

=== PAST MEDICAL HISTORY ===
Generally healthy. Mild seasonal allergies, no known drug allergies. Born full term at home (lay midwife) by uncomplicated SVD. Followed by family practice, mostly well-child visits.

=== IMMUNIZATIONS ===
This is the salient piece of the history. Per parental report and confirmed against the LA SHOTS registry, Lila received MMR #1 at 18 months but never received MMR #2. The family has religious exemption on file with the school district for all subsequent recommended immunizations. No DTaP boosters after age 4. No Tdap. No Tdap-IPV. No varicella series. No HPV.

=== FAMILY HISTORY ===
Mother — alive 38, GERD. Father — alive 41, otherwise healthy. Older sibling, age 14, healthy and similarly partially immunized. No family history of malignancy, autoimmune disease, immunodeficiency, or congenital syndromes.

=== SOCIAL HISTORY ===
Lives with biological parents and one sibling in a single-family home in Algiers Point. Attends a small homeschool co-op with approximately 18 other children, several of whom are also incompletely immunized. No tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drug exposures in the home. Family pets: one dog, one cat, indoor only, both up to date on rabies. No raw milk, no unusual food exposures. Recent travel as above.

=== REVIEW OF SYSTEMS ===
Constitutional: positive for fever, fatigue, decreased appetite
HEENT: positive for coryza, conjunctivitis, photophobia, sore throat
Resp: positive for cough
GI: negative for vomiting, negative for diarrhea
GU: decreased urine output by report
Skin: positive for rash as described in HPI
Neuro: negative for headache, negative for neck stiffness, negative for altered mental status, negative for focal weakness, negative for seizure activity
MSK: negative for joint pain or swelling

=== PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ===
Vitals: Temp 39.6C oral, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 98% room air, BP 102/64
General: tired-appearing girl in NAD, lying in bed in a darkened room
HEENT: bilateral conjunctival injection without exudate. Photophobia present. Bluish-white spots ~1 mm on buccal mucosa opposite lower molars. Posterior pharynx erythematous. Tympanic membranes normal bilaterally.
Neck: supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy
Lungs: scattered expiratory wheezes; no rales or rhonchi; no respiratory distress
Cardiac: tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmur
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly
Skin: erythematous, blanching, maculopapular rash on hairline, face, posterior auricular area, neck, anterior chest, upper back, and proximal arms; spreading caudally; no vesicles, no petechiae
Neuro: alert, fully oriented, age-appropriate. CN II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 globally. Sensation intact. Cerebellar exam unremarkable.

=== LABORATORY DATA ===
CBC: WBC 4.2 (low), with absolute lymphopenia
CMP: unremarkable
CRP: 24 mg/L
Measles IgM: pending, send-out
Nasopharyngeal RT-PCR for measles RNA: pending, send-out

=== ASSESSMENT AND PLAN ===
11-year-old female with classic febrile prodrome (fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis), Koplik spots on buccal mucosa, and the characteristic descending maculopapular rash, in the context of recent travel to a region with documented measles transmission and incomplete MMR coverage. The clinical presentation is essentially diagnostic for measles. We will await laboratory confirmation but the public health and infection control implications are sufficient that we are managing as a confirmed case from this point.

Final admission diagnosis: Measles (rubeola), suspected import-related case, post-exposure undervaccinated.

Management:
1. Airborne isolation, negative pressure room
2. Supportive care — IV fluids, antipyretics, dim room for photophobia
3. Vitamin A 200,000 IU PO once today and again tomorrow per WHO guidance for severe measles in children >12 months
4. Public health notification initiated to LDH; reporting per LA reportable disease rules
5. Contact tracing for exposed individuals at the homeschool co-op and on the recent flight to be coordinated with LDH epidemiology
6. Daily exam, monitoring for measles complications (otitis media, pneumonia, encephalitis)

Vitals at admission as documented above.

description: Adv6 axis=code injection bait: clinical prose contains a literal 'SNOMED 99999999' string in a quoted internet-rumor sentence. Probes whether the inline-regex tier (SNOMED_RE) emits the bait code. The actual diagnosis is acute hepatitis B (curated SNOMED 66071002), with HBsAg+ (5193-8) and entecavir (105078). The bait substring is in the form '(SNOMED 99999999)' inside a single-quoted citation that the patient read on the internet — should NOT extract because... actually it WILL extract from the inline regex regardless of context. The case documents this expected over-fire — the SNOMED regex is purely lexical with no NegEx scope check on inline tier.
